stem: Let \( g(x) = \lfloor x \rfloor + ([[a]]) \), where \( \lfloor \cdot \rfloor \) denotes the greatest-integer (floor) function. Evaluate \( \lim_{x \to [[n]]^{-}} g(x) \).
solution: As \( x \) approaches the integer \( [[n]] \) from below, \( \lfloor x \rfloor = [[n]] - 1 \). Hence \( g(x) \to ([[n]] - 1) + ([[a]]) = [[answer]] \). (From the right the value would be \( [[n]] + ([[a]]) \).)
distractor_rationales: This is the right-hand limit, where \( \lfloor x \rfloor = n \).
This drops the added constant \( a \).
This uses the point itself and ignores that the floor drops by one from the left.
This keeps only the constant \( a \).
This is off by one from the correct one-sided limit.
This is off by one from the correct one-sided limit.

stem: Let \( g(x) = \lfloor x \rfloor \), the greatest-integer (floor) function. Since \( [[c]] \) is NOT an integer, \( g \) is continuous there. Evaluate \( \lim_{x \to [[c]]} g(x) \).
solution: Since \( [[c]] \) lies strictly between the consecutive integers \( [[n]] \) and \( [[n]]+1 \), the floor function is constant (equal to \( [[n]] \)) on an open interval around \( [[c]] \), so it is continuous there and the two-sided limit equals \( \lfloor [[c]] \rfloor = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This rounds up to the next integer instead of taking the floor.
This is off by one below the correct integer part.
This doubles the integer part.
This ignores the actual value of \( c \) entirely.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.

solution: As \( x \to \left(\frac{[[m]]}{[[k]]}\right)^{-} \), the quantity \( [[k]]x \) approaches the integer \( [[m]] \) from below, so \( \lfloor [[k]]x \rfloor = [[m]] - 1 \) just before the boundary. Hence \( h(x) \to [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the right-hand limit, where \( \lfloor [[k]]x \rfloor = m \) exactly.
This is one more than the right-hand limit.
This multiplies instead of recognizing the scaled variable already hits the integer \( m \).
This divides \( m \) by \( k \) again, undoing the scaling.
This is off by one from the correct one-sided limit.
This is off by two from the correct one-sided limit.
This is off by one (other direction) from the correct one-sided limit.

solution: Using \( \dfrac{[[b]]}{x} - 1 < \left\lfloor \dfrac{[[b]]}{x} \right\rfloor \le \dfrac{[[b]]}{x} \), multiplying through by \( [[a]]x > 0 \) and squeezing as \( x \to 0^{+} \) gives \( [[a]]x \left\lfloor \dfrac{[[b]]}{x} \right\rfloor \to [[a]]\cdot[[b]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds the two constants instead of multiplying them.
This keeps only the coefficient outside the floor.
This keeps only the constant inside the floor.
This divides the constants instead of multiplying them.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.

stem: Let \( r(x) = [[b]] + \left(x - \lfloor x \rfloor\right) \), a shift of the fractional-part (sawtooth) function. Evaluate \( \lim_{x \to [[n]]^{-}} r(x) \).
solution: Just below the integer \( [[n]] \), \( \lfloor x \rfloor = [[n]]-1 \), so \( x - \lfloor x \rfloor \to [[n]] - ([[n]]-1) = 1 \). Hence \( r(x) \to [[b]] + 1 = [[answer]] \). (Just above \( [[n]] \), \( r(x) \to [[b]] \) instead, so the two-sided limit does not exist.)
distractor_rationales: This uses the point itself plus the shift instead of the fractional-part contribution.
This subtracts instead of adding the fractional-part contribution of 1.
This drops the shift \( b \) entirely.
This is the right-hand limit, not the requested left-hand limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by two from the correct limit.
